skill.name: airunway-aks-setup
skill.description: Guides users through AI Runway setup on an existing AKS cluster, including cluster checks, controller installation, GPU assessment, provider setup, model deployment, and smoke testing.
skill.summary: Set up AI Runway on AKS from cluster verification to a running model deployment.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, filesystem, weak-crypto, and network patterns, but review found they are Markdown setup examples rather than hidden executable code. The weak cryptography hits are false positives from model dtype terms such as bfloat16 and float16, while the command and network examples are legitimate AKS operations that can modify live clusters and expose tokens if misused. Publish with an operational-risk warning and require user confirmation before install, deploy, secret, or network actions.

medium_findings.title: Cluster-Mutating Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to install CRDs, deploy controllers, deploy providers, create Kubernetes secrets, apply ModelDeployment resources, and run rollback commands. These are expected for an AKS setup skill, but they can change live infrastructure and incur GPU costs if executed without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines directly show commands that mutate Kubernetes resources. The surrounding skill text requires user confirmation, so this is operational risk rather than malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Secret Handling Requires Careful Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill provides instructions for reading a HuggingFace token, writing it to a temporary file, and creating a Kubernetes secret. The Bash flow removes the temporary file on exit and sets restrictive permissions, but mishandling could expose a model access token.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code examples explicitly handle a user token and store it in a Kubernetes secret. The guidance includes cleanup, but token exposure remains a realistic misuse risk.

medium_findings.title: Network Calls To Model Endpoint
medium_findings.description: The smoke test sends a request to the deployed model endpoint using curl or Invoke-RestMethod. This is expected validation behavior, but the endpoint value is cluster-derived and should be reviewed before sending data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network calls are plainly documented smoke tests, not covert exfiltration. Risk depends on the endpoint returned by the cluster and the prompt content used for testing.

low_findings.title: Static Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak-cryptography alerts appear to match model precision and hardware compatibility terms such as bfloat16 and float16, not cryptographic algorithms. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or custom cryptography use in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows GPU dtype and troubleshooting text, not cryptographic implementation. The static pattern label does not match the semantic context.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal And Device File Findings Are Documentation Links Or Redirects
low_findings.description: The path traversal findings are caused by relative Markdown links to neighboring reference files, and the device-file findings are shell redirection to /dev/null. These are benign in documentation context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited paths are Markdown reference links or normal command-output suppression. I found no evidence of untrusted path input or file traversal logic.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill contains many kubectl, make, shell, and PowerShell examples. They are not automatically executed by the skill, but they perform real cluster operations when copied or run by an agent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are present as fenced Markdown examples. Their impact is clear, but execution requires user or agent action outside the report itself.

dangerous_patterns.title: Kubernetes Resource Apply And Delete Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The deployment and rollback sections include kubectl apply and delete commands. These are appropriate for setup and rollback, but they can create or remove resources in a live namespace.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections show direct Kubernetes manifest application and cleanup commands. This is intended behavior for the skill, not evidence of malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Setting up AI model serving on AKS requires many cluster, GPU, provider, and deployment checks. This skill gives a guided workflow for installing AI Runway components and validating a first model deployment.
actual_capabilities: Verifies required CLI tools and the active Kubernetes context before setup.
Checks AKS nodes, GPU labels, GPU capacity, and supported model precision constraints.
Guides AI Runway controller and CRD installation from the repository root.
Recommends KAITO, Dynamo, or KubeRay based on hardware and serving needs.
Creates starter ModelDeployment manifests for gated and non-gated HuggingFace models.
Provides rollout checks, smoke tests, troubleshooting steps, and rollback commands.
limitations: Requires an existing AKS cluster and local access to kubectl, make, and curl.
Does not provision the AKS cluster or GPU node pool itself.
Model recommendations may need adjustment for quota, model access, and current provider images.
Commands can change live cluster resources and should be reviewed before execution.

use_cases.title: Onboard an AKS cluster
use_cases.description: Verify cluster readiness, install AI Runway components, and confirm controller health before opening model deployment to teams.

use_cases.title: Choose a starter inference path
use_cases.description: Map available GPU hardware to a provider and model size that should fit the cluster capacity.

use_cases.title: Recover from deployment issues
use_cases.description: Use status checks, logs, describe commands, and rollback steps to diagnose failed model or provider deployments.

prompt_templates.title: Verify my cluster
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the AI Runway AKS setup skill to verify my current Kubernetes context, node inventory, and GPU availability. Stop before making changes.

prompt_templates.title: Install the controller
prompt_templates.prompt: Guide me through AI Runway controller installation on my AKS cluster. Ask before each command that changes cluster state.

prompt_templates.title: Pick a provider and model
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my detected GPU types and recommend the AI Runway provider and starter model that best fit my cluster.

prompt_templates.title: Deploy and validate a model
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me deploy a gated HuggingFace model with AI Runway on AKS, create the token secret safely, monitor readiness, and run a smoke test.

output_examples.input: Verify my existing AKS cluster for AI Runway setup.
output_examples.output: Cluster context, node count, GPU inventory, missing prerequisites, and a clear stop or proceed recommendation.

output_examples.input: Recommend a first model for one T4 GPU.
output_examples.output: A T4-compatible model recommendation, dtype warning, preferred provider, and notes about expected memory headroom.

output_examples.input: My ModelDeployment is not Ready after ten minutes.
output_examples.output: A diagnostic checklist covering deployment status, events, pod logs, provider readiness, image pulls, token configuration, and rollback options.

best_practices: Confirm the target cluster, namespace, and expected GPU cost before installing or deploying resources.
Use non-gated starter models first when validating a new AI Runway installation.
Review generated manifests and secret commands before applying them to a live cluster.
anti_patterns: Do not run install, deploy, or rollback commands without explicit user approval.
Do not paste HuggingFace tokens into visible shell history or shared logs.
Do not assume bfloat16 support on older GPUs such as T4 or V100.

faq.question: Does this skill create an AKS cluster?
faq.answer: No. It assumes an AKS cluster already exists and focuses on AI Runway setup and model deployment.

faq.question: Which inference provider does it recommend first?
faq.answer: It defaults to KAITO for most users unless the workload needs Dynamo or an existing Ray ecosystem.

faq.question: Can it support CPU-only clusters?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a CPU-only path using KAITO with llama.cpp and a small quantized model.

faq.question: Does it handle HuggingFace gated models?
faq.answer: Yes. It shows how to create a Kubernetes secret from a HuggingFace token for gated model access.

faq.question: Why is the security risk medium?
faq.answer: The skill is not malicious, but its commands can modify live clusters, handle secrets, and send network requests.

faq.question: What tools must be installed locally?
faq.answer: The workflow expects kubectl, make, and curl, plus valid Kubernetes credentials for the target AKS cluster.
